comparison_method: intersect Paper 3 holdout IDs with Paper 2 per-id refusal judgments
expansion_strategy: translate fresh HarmBench EN -> RO + paraphrase RoSafetyBench train items 3x via Llama-3.3-70B + paraphrase + regenerate over-refusal stream 3x via Llama-3.3-70B + dedupe by exact prompt text
headline_finding: All three anchors trained to in-distribution convergence on the expanded preference set (~434 pairs/anchor, 2.17x the e6 baseline). Held-out cross-lingual refusal moved -3.5 pp (Qwen), +1.2 pp (Llama), -3.5 pp (Gemma) relative to base; on every anchor the recipe is non-positive on cross-lingual at both data scales. Toxicity recovered partially (Qwen +5.6 pp, Llama +1.9 pp, Gemma +1.9 pp from the e6 baseline). Jailbreak recovered substantially on Qwen and Llama. Cross-lingual did not respond to data scale; toxicity and jailbreak did.
implications: Data scale is not the dominant bottleneck for cross-lingual refusal at LoRA r=16. Across two pair counts (200 and ~434) the cross-lingual delta stays in the [-7, +1.2] pp band on every anchor.
In-distribution-similar OOD (toxicity, jailbreak) responds to data scale: the x4 expansion recovered 5-15 pp on these dimensions vs the e6 baseline.

Truly OOD (cross-lingual) does not respond. The held-out cross-lingual prompts are HarmBench-RO equivalents in our holdout split; the recipe trains on RoSafetyBench-RO + paraphrases + HarmBench-RO translations but the trained policy does not generalise to the held-out cross-lingual partition.

Reading: at LoRA r=16 with DPO loss, the trained policy fits the in-distribution preference contrast but cannot bridge the cultural-linguistic distribution gap to the held-out cross-lingual evaluation. Larger-rank LoRA, full-model DPO, or alternative preference losses (KTO/ORPO/SimPO) may be needed; we do not test those here.
